sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 3, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: The player moved RIGHT action was executed. Let me check where the player moved to. Let me compare the before/after frames.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Find player position before and after RIGHT
before = last_transition.before_frame.segmentation
after = current_frame.segmentation
# Find the blue square with black dot (the player)
def find_player(seg):
    for n in seg['nodes']:
        if n['color'] == 'b' and n['children']:
            for c in n['children']:
                child_node = [x for x in seg['nodes'] if x['id'] == c][0]
                if child_node['color'] == 'B':
                    return n
    return None
bp = find_player(before)
ap = find_player(after)
print(f"Player before: {bp['boundary'] if bp else 'None'}")
print(f"Player after: {ap['boundary'] if ap else 'None'}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content: The previous `python` call inspected the frame but did not execute `action(...)`. Reply with a `python` tool call only; do not write assistant text before it. Do not print another broad segmentation dump. In the code, compute only the minimum extra evidence needed, choose the best probe or move, and call `action(...)` before the snippet ends. If `MOUSE` is valid, use integer `row` and `col`.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The player didn't move! The player is still at [[14,14] to [18,18]. The RIGHT action didn't move the player. So the player is blocked on the right side. Let me check the maze layout again.
Looking at the ascii from earlier:
Row 14: BbbbbbBWWWWWBBBBBBBWWWWWWWBRBWWWWWWWW
The player is at cols 14-18 (bbbbb). To the right is B (col 19), then W (cols 20-24), then B (cols 25-31), then W (cols 32-38), then B (col 39), R (col 40), B (col 41), W (cols 42-49)
Wait, the player is at cols 14-18, and there's a B wall at col 19. So the player is blocked on the right.
But looking at the image, the player is in a blue square at the top-left of the maze. The white path is to the right of the player. Let me re-examine the ascii more carefully.
Actually, looking at the image, the player is in the top-left corner of the maze. The white path goes to the right and down from there. But the ascii shows a B wall between the player and the white path.
Wait, let me re-examine. The player is a 4x4 blue square. In the ascii, cols 14-18 are 14-17 (4 columns). Col 19 is B (wall. Cols 20-24 are W (white path).
So the player is blocked by a wall on the right. But looking at the image, the player is in the top-left corner of the maze, and there's a white path to the right. Let me re-examine the ascii.
Actually, wait - the player moved DOWN from [8,14]-[12,18] to [14,14]-[18,18]. The initial position was [8,14]-[12,18]. After DOWN, it's [14,14]-[18,18]. After RIGHT, it's still [14,14]-[18,18].
So the player can move DOWN but not RIGHT from the current position. Let me try DOWN again.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
action(['DOWN'])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"action_semantics": {"DOWN": "moves player down 6px", "RIGHT": "blocked on right"}, "object_taxonomy": {"b_with_B_child": "player (blue square with black dot)", "b_19px": "goal (blue U-shape at bottom-right)", "R": "red path (obstacle?)", "W": "white path (walkable)", "B": "black walls"}, "hud_map": {"bottom_bar": "blue bar at row 63, likely timer"}, "win_pattern": "navigate player to goal", "level_log": [], "cross_level_notes": "", "world_model": "maze with player at [14,14]-[18,18], goal at [49,43]-[55,49], white path is walkable, black is walls, red is obstacle", "goal_model": "navigate player from player to goal via white path", "recent_findings": "player moves 6px per action, RIGHT blocked at current position", "open_questions": "can player move DOWN further? what is the path?", "current_plan": "try DOWN again, then RIGHT when possible", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": []}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
